preamble: The transformation from a voiced postalveolar affricate (d͡ʒ) to a voiced palatal approximant (j) is a common instance of lenition, a sound change where a consonant becomes 'weaker' or less constricted. This process involves the deaffrication of the sound, moving from a stop-fricative combination to a pure approximant, often in specific phonetic environments or as a dialectal variant.

examples.note: The word 'dagger' undergoes lenition in Hadari Kuwaiti Spoken Arabic.
The word 'drawer' undergoes lenition in Hadari Kuwaiti Spoken Arabic.

sources: AlOtaibi, D., AlQenaie, S. D., & Park, S. (2022). Lenition in Kuwaiti Arabic: An Optimality Theory Approach. Jordan Journal of Modern Languages and Literatures, 14(3), 517-533.
Alqarni, A. (2017). Dialect Contact and Koineisation in Riyadh: A study on the Dialect of Alqarni Tribe. PhD thesis, University of Essex.
Johnstone, T. M. (1967). Eastern Arabian Dialect Studies. Oxford University Press.
Mustafawi, E. (2006). Lexical and sociolinguistic variation in Qatari Arabic. PhD diss., University of Edinburgh.
